paragraphs.text:
# Mercury Complexes in SRD-46 — An Exploration 🧪

Mercury is one of the most fascinating metals in coordination chemistry, with a rich dataset in NIST SRD-46. Here's a comprehensive overview based on the database.

---

## Mercury Species in SRD-46

| Species | Formula | Charge | β Definitions | Ligand Partners | Complex Records |
|---------|---------|--------|--------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Hg(II)** | Hg²⁺ | +2 | 68 | 310 | 1,097 |
| **Methylmercury** | MeHg⁺ | +1 | 18 | 79 | 148 |
| **Hg(I)** | Hg⁺ | +1 | 12 | 31 | 83 |
| **Phenylmercury** | PhHg⁺ | +1 | 2 | 8 | 8 |
| **Ethylmercury** | EtHg⁺ | +1 | 4 | 3 | 6 |

**Hg²⁺ dominates** the dataset with over 1,000 measured stability constants across 310 ligand types — reflecting its exceptional importance in environmental and analytical chemistry.

paragraphs.text:
## Top Ligand Systems for Hg²⁺

The catalog reveals a clear hierarchy of ligand families, ordered by data richness:

| Rank | Ligand | Class | # VLMs | Max Stoichiometry | T Range (°C) | I Range (M) |
|------|--------|-------|--------|------------------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | **Chloride** (Cl⁻) | Halide | 38 | HgCl₄²⁻ | 19–30 | 0–3.15 |
| 2 | **Cyanide** (CN⁻) | Pseudohalide | 37 | Hg(CN)₄²⁻ | 5–45 | 0–2.15 |
| 3 | **Hydroxide** (OH⁻) | Hydrolysis | 29 | Hg₃(OH)₆ | 19–30 | 0–3.15 |
| 4 | **Bromide** (Br⁻) | Halide | 28 | HgBr₄²⁻ | 16.5–31.5 | 0.275–3.225 |
| 5 | **Thiourea** | S-donor | 27 | Hg(tu)₄²⁺ | 19–30 | 0–1.15 |
| 6 | **Thiocyanate** (SCN⁻) | S-donor | 20 | Hg(SCN)₄²⁻ | 19–30 | 0–1.15 |
| 7 | **Iodide** (I⁻) | Halide | 16 | HgI₄²⁻ | 16.5–31.5 | 0.275–0.725 |
| 8 | **Ethylenediamine** (en) | N-donor | 13 | Hg(en)₂²⁺ | 19–30 | 0–3.15 |
| 9 | **Ammonia** (NH₃) | N-donor | 12 | Hg(NH₃)₄²⁺ | 19–30 | 0–2.15 |
| 10 | **EDTA** | Polyaminocarboxylate | 11 | HgEDTA²⁻ | 19–30 | 0–1.15 |
| 11 | **Azide** (N₃⁻) | Pseudohalide | 10 | Hg(N₃)₂ | 19–30 | 0–1.15 |
| 12 | **Carbonate** | Oxoanion | 10 | Hg₃(CO₃)₅ | 19–30 | 0–3.15 |
| 13 | **Imidazole** | N-heterocycle | 10 | Hg(im)₂²⁺ | 19–30 | 0–3.15 |
| 14 | **Hydrogen sulfide** (H₂S) | S-donor | 8 | HgS (solid) | 15–30 | 0–1.15 |
| 15 | **Pyridine** | N-heterocycle | 8 | Hg(py)₄²⁺ | 16.5–31.5 | 0.275–0.725 |

paragraphs.text:
## Key Chemistry Insights

### 🔴 Hg²⁺ is a Classic "Soft" Lewis Acid (HSAB Theory)
Mercury(II) shows a dramatic preference for **soft donor atoms** in the order:
> **S >> I > Br > Cl >> N > O**

This is why HgS (cinnabar) is extraordinarily insoluble (log Ksp ≈ −52), and why thiourea, thiocyanate, and sulfide ligands dominate the strongest complexes. The cyanide system (37 VLMs, up to Hg(CN)₄²⁻) is also exceptionally strong due to CN⁻'s soft character.

claims.text: Hg²⁺ is a Classic "Soft" Lewis Acid
Mercury(II) shows a dramatic preference for **soft donor atoms**
S >> I > Br > Cl >> N > O
thiourea, thiocyanate, and sulfide ligands dominate the strongest complexes.
The cyanide system (37 VLMs, up to Hg(CN)₄²⁻) is also exceptionally strong

paragraphs.text:
### 🟡 Halide Complexes — A Rich Series
Hg²⁺ forms the complete halide series HgX⁺, HgX₂, HgX₃⁻, HgX₄²⁻ for Cl, Br, and I, with stability increasing I > Br > Cl. The chloride system alone has 38 measured values across ionic strengths from near-zero to 3.15 M — critical for understanding mercury speciation in seawater and industrial brines.

claims.text: Hg²⁺ forms the complete halide series HgX⁺, HgX₂, HgX₃⁻, HgX₄²⁻
with stability increasing I > Br > Cl.
The chloride system alone has 38 measured values
across ionic strengths from near-zero to 3.15 M
— critical for understanding mercury speciation in seawater and industrial brines.

paragraphs.text:
### 🟢 Organomercury Cations (MeHg⁺, EtHg⁺, PhHg⁺)
Methylmercury (MeHg⁺) is the most data-rich organomercury species with 79 ligand partners and 148 complexes. It forms linear 2-coordinate complexes and has an even stronger preference for sulfur donors than Hg²⁺ — explaining why it bioaccumulates by binding to cysteine thiol groups in proteins. This is the species responsible for Minamata disease.

claims.text: is the most data-rich organomercury species
has an even stronger preference for sulfur donors than Hg²⁺
by binding to cysteine thiol groups in proteins.
This is the species responsible for Minamata disease.

paragraphs.text:
### 🔵 Hydrolysis Network
The hydroxide system shows complex polynuclear species including Hg₂(OH)²⁺, Hg₃(OH)₃³⁺, and even solid HgO (red and black polymorphs), measured across 29 VLMs. This is important for understanding mercury behavior at near-neutral to alkaline pH.

claims.text: The hydroxide system shows complex polynuclear species
including Hg₂(OH)²⁺, Hg₃(OH)₃³⁺, and even solid HgO (red and black polymorphs)
This is important for understanding mercury behavior at near-neutral to alkaline pH.

paragraphs.text:
## Notable Exotic Ligands
- **Selenourea** — 9 VLMs, showing Hg²⁺ affinity for Se donors (Se is even softer than S)
- **Thiosemicarbazide** — 9 VLMs, a bifunctional S/N ligand
- **TDS** (a hexacarboxylate ether) — 8 VLMs with 8 different species types, the most complex speciation model in the dataset
- **β-Thiodiglycol** — a thioether diol with 12 VLMs, relevant to chemical warfare agent hydrolysis chemistry

---

*All data sourced from NIST SRD-46 (NIST Standard Reference Database 46 — NIST Critically Selected Stability Constants of Metal Complexes).*

> 💡 **Want to dig deeper?** I can look up specific log β values for any of these systems, compare Hg²⁺ vs. MeHg⁺ affinities for sulfur ligands, explore the halide speciation network, or find the original literature citations for any measurement.
